title: f-strings — the modern way
instruction:
In Chapter 2, you printed variables separately. But real programs need to embed variables inside sentences.

f-strings do that. Put `f` before the opening quote, then use `{variable}` anywhere inside:

`f"Hello, {name}!"` — Python replaces `{name}` with the variable's value.

Run this.
starter:
name = "Alex"
age = 25
print(f"Hello, {name}!")
print(f"You are {age} years old.")
successMessage: f-strings embed variables directly in text. No str() conversion, no + concatenation — just put the variable in curly braces. This is the modern Python way.

title: Expressions inside f-strings
instruction:
The `{}` in an f-string can hold any Python expression — not just variable names.

`f"{2 + 2}"` → `"4"`
`f"{name.upper()}"` → the name in uppercase

Create an f-string that shows the tax-included price (multiply price by 1.2 inside the `{}`).
starter:
price = 100
product = "Laptop"
print(f"Product: {product}")
# TODO: print price with 20% tax using an expression inside {}

codeChecks.message: Multiply price by 1.2 inside the f-string: {price * 1.2}
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Any valid Python expression goes inside {}. Math, method calls, comparisons — they all work inline.

title: Format numbers — decimal places
instruction:
Floats sometimes print with too many decimal places: `3.141592653589793`.

Control it with format specifiers: `{value:.2f}` means 2 decimal places.

Fix the output below to show prices with exactly 2 decimal places.
starter:
price1 = 9.9
price2 = 14.99999
price3 = 100.0

# TODO: print each price with exactly 2 decimal places
print(f"Item 1: {price1}")
print(f"Item 2: {price2}")
print(f"Item 3: {price3}")
successMessage: :.2f = float with 2 decimal places. Always use this for prices and currency. 15.00 looks much better than 14.99999.

title: Format numbers — thousands separator
instruction:
`1000000` is hard to read. `1,000,000` is clear.

Use `{value:,}` for thousands separators.

Add separators to the three big numbers below.
starter:
population = 8000000000
salary = 120000
distance = 384400

# TODO: print each with thousands separators
print(f"World population: {population}")
print(f"Annual salary: {salary}")
print(f"Distance to Moon: {distance} km")
successMessage: :, adds thousands separators automatically. Much clearer than 8000000000. You can combine them: {salary:,.2f} for currency.

title: String width and alignment
instruction:
For neatly aligned columns, you can set a minimum width:

`{value:<10}` — left-align in 10 characters
`{value:>10}` — right-align in 10 characters
`{value:^10}` — center in 10 characters

Run this to see the alignment in action.
starter:
items = [("Apple", 0.99), ("Banana", 0.49), ("Mango", 2.50)]

print(f"{'Item':<10} {'Price':>8}")
print("-" * 20)
for name, price in items:
    print(f"{name:<10} {price:>8.2f}")
successMessage: Column-aligned output. < for left, > for right, ^ for center. The number is the minimum width. Use this for tables, reports, or any structured output.

title: print() — sep and end
instruction:
`print()` has two optional parameters:

- `sep` — what goes between multiple values (default: space)
- `end` — what goes at the end of the line (default: newline `\n`)

Run this. Then add two print calls that use `sep="-"` and `end=" | "`.
starter:
print("A", "B", "C")
print("A", "B", "C", sep=", ")
# TODO: print with sep="-" and then with end=" | "

codeChecks.message: Add a print with a custom end parameter.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: sep and end give you control over exactly how print formats output. Print CSV-ready lines, inline sequences, or progress indicators.

title: Escape sequences
instruction:
Some characters cannot go directly in a string. Use escape sequences:

- `\n` — newline
- `\t` — tab
- `\\` — literal backslash
- `\"` — quote inside a double-quoted string

Run this. Then add a line that prints a Windows-style file path using `\\`.
starter:
print("Line 1\nLine 2\nLine 3")
print("Name:\tAlex")
print("She said \"hello\"")
# TODO: print a Windows file path like C:\Users\Alex

codeChecks.message: Use \\\\ for a literal backslash in a file path.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Escape sequences are Python's way of putting special characters in strings. \n and \t are the most common — you will use them constantly.

title: Raw strings — r""
instruction:
If you have a string with many backslashes (like file paths or regex patterns), raw strings save you:

`r"C:\Users\Alex"` — backslashes are treated literally. No escape needed.

Fix the broken file path below using a raw string.
starter:
# BUG: \U and \A are not valid escape sequences — use a raw string
path = "C:\Users\Alex\Documents"
print(path)
successMessage: r"" raw strings treat every backslash literally. Essential for Windows paths, file patterns, and regex — anywhere you have lots of backslashes.

title: Multi-line strings — triple quotes
instruction:
Triple quotes `"""` or `'''` let you write strings that span multiple lines without `\n`.

Perfect for long messages, SQL queries, HTML templates, or docstrings.

Run this. Then write your own multi-line message.
starter:
message = """Dear Alex,

Welcome to Python.
Things are about to get interesting.

Cheers,
The Python Team"""

print(message)
successMessage: Triple-quoted strings preserve all whitespace and newlines exactly as written. Great for long text — and they double as docstrings for documenting functions.

title: .format() — the older way
instruction:
Before f-strings (Python 3.6+), `.format()` was the standard:

`"Hello, {}!".format(name)`

You will see this in old code. It works the same way as f-strings.

Rewrite the two f-string lines below using `.format()` instead.
starter:
name = "Alex"
age = 25

# TODO: rewrite these as .format() calls
print(f"Hello, {name}!")
print(f"You are {age} years old.")

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: .format() still works perfectly. f-strings are cleaner and faster — use f-strings in new code, but recognise .format() in old code.

title: % formatting — the oldest way
instruction:
Even older than `.format()` is `%` formatting:

`"Hello, %s!" % name` — `%s` is replaced by the string value.
`"Age: %d" % age` — `%d` is replaced by an integer.

Run this. Then write a line that formats a price using `%` formatting with `%.2f`.
starter:
name = "Alex"
age = 25
price = 19.9

print("Hello, %s!" % name)
print("Age: %d" % age)
# TODO: print price using %.2f format

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: % formatting is Python's oldest string formatting. Still works, rarely used in new code. You will see it in legacy codebases — and now you understand it.

title: Build a receipt
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a formatted receipt that prints:
```
Receipt
---------
Coffee         $3.50
Sandwich      $7.25
Total         $10.75
---------
Thank you!
```

Use f-strings with `:.2f` for prices and `:<` or `:>` for alignment.
successMessage: A real formatted receipt. Decimal-aligned prices, padded columns — this is exactly how real programs format financial output. Chapter 4 done.
